glossary.def: The geometric decay or blow-up of the backward signal in a deep network, because the gradient at a layer is a product of one Jacobian per layer (EQ N1.2). When the typical singular value is below 1 the product shrinks to zero and early layers stop learning; above 1 it explodes and training diverges. Init, normalization, and residuals all exist to keep that product near 1.

A variance-preserving weight init that sets Var(W) = 2/(n_in + n_out), the harmonic compromise between keeping forward and backward variance at 1. Derived assuming a roughly linear activation around zero, so it is the right default for symmetric, zero-centered nonlinearities such as tanh.

The ReLU-specific init that sets Var(W) = 2/n_in (std = sqrt(2/n_in)). The extra factor of 2 over 1/n_in exactly cancels ReLU's variance-halving, so activation variance is preserved through depth. It is the framework default for ReLU-family networks.

A layer that standardizes each feature channel to zero mean and unit variance using mini-batch statistics, then re-scales and re-shifts with learned parameters gamma and beta (EQ N1.6). It permits higher learning rates, reduces sensitivity to initialization, and mildly regularizes; at inference it switches to running-average statistics.

The drift in the distribution of a layer's inputs as the layers below it update during training — the motivation Ioffe & Szegedy gave for batch normalization. Later work (Santurkar et al., 2018) argued BatchNorm's real benefit is a smoother loss landscape rather than reducing this shift; the mechanism remains debated.

A shortcut that adds a block's input to its output, h_{l+1} = h_l + F(h_l) (EQ N1.7), so the block learns only a residual correction. Differentiating gives I + dF/dh, an additive identity path that lets the gradient reach early layers undiminished — the idea behind ResNets and the Transformer residual stream.

A regularizer that randomly zeros each activation with probability p during training and rescales the survivors by 1/(1-p) (inverted dropout, EQ N1.9). Each step trains a different sub-network; at test time the full network behaves as an implicit ensemble. It prevents units from co-adapting and so forces redundant, robust features.

An L2 penalty (lambda/2)·sum(w^2) that shrinks every weight toward zero each step (EQ N1.10), biasing the model toward smoother, lower-variance functions. With adaptive optimizers, classical L2 and true weight decay differ because the gradient is rescaled; AdamW decouples the decay from the adaptive step and is the modern default.

refs.cite: Glorot, X. & Bengio, Y. (2010). Understanding the difficulty of training deep feedforward neural networks. AISTATS.
He, K., Zhang, X., Ren, S. & Sun, J. (2015). Delving Deep into Rectifiers: Surpassing Human-Level Performance on ImageNet Classification. ICCV.
Ioffe, S. & Szegedy, C. (2015). Batch Normalization: Accelerating Deep Network Training by Reducing Internal Covariate Shift. ICML.
He, K., Zhang, X., Ren, S. & Sun, J. (2016). Deep Residual Learning for Image Recognition. CVPR.
Srivastava, N., Hinton, G., Krizhevsky, A., Sutskever, I. & Salakhutdinov, R. (2014). Dropout: A Simple Way to Prevent Neural Networks from Overfitting. JMLR 15.
Loshchilov, I. & Hutter, F. (2019). Decoupled Weight Decay Regularization. ICLR.
Santurkar, S., Tsipras, D., Ilyas, A. & Madry, A. (2018). How Does Batch Normalization Help Optimization? NeurIPS.
